title: Motivation Is a Feeling. Systems Are Infrastructure.
excerpt: Motivation shows up when it feels like it. A system does not ask permission. What a 66-day habit study and one solo marathon taught me about the difference.
body:
I ran a marathon with no race number, no start gun, and no one waiting at the finish line. 26.21 kilometres, self-organised, on my own route, in 4 hours 38 minutes and 32 seconds. Nobody was tracking me. There was no crowd to perform for and no medal to justify the pain.

People ask what motivated me to finish. Wrong question. By kilometre 15 there was no motivation left — my legs weren't excited, my mind wasn't inspired, my body was actively lobbying to stop. What kept me moving was not a feeling. It was a structure I had already built, in training, months before that morning: wake, hydrate, stretch, move, don't negotiate with the first ten minutes. The marathon didn't need motivation. It just needed me to execute the system I'd already rehearsed a hundred times.

## Motivation is weather. Systems are infrastructure.

Weather changes by the hour. Some days you feel sharp and hungry to work. Other days you feel like a wet towel. If your output depends on the weather, your output will be as inconsistent as the sky.

Infrastructure doesn't care about weather. A bridge does not ask how it feels before holding your weight. A system built into your day — same trigger, same action, every time — carries you through the days motivation forgot to show up.

I learned this the hard way, first through calisthenics and long-distance running, then by stress-testing the idea against the core philosophies of fifty different thinkers over six months. Whatever survived that filter had one thing in common: none of them relied on how I felt in the moment.

## What the research actually says

I didn't invent this idea, and I'm not going to pretend I did. A 2010 study out of University College London tracked ninety-six people trying to build a new daily habit — an eating, drinking, or activity behaviour tied to an existing routine, like doing something right after breakfast. Researchers measured how automatic the behaviour became over twelve weeks.

The number people love to quote is 66 days — the median time it took to reach near-peak automaticity. But the detail that actually matters is the range: it took anywhere from 18 to 254 days, depending on the person and the behaviour. There's no universal deadline where a habit magically clicks into place. What mattered more than the timeline was consistency of context — doing the thing in the same situation, attached to the same cue, over and over. Missing one day barely dented the process. Being inconsistent about the trigger did the real damage.

That matches exactly what I saw in my own training. The days I tied the workout to a fixed anchor — right after the morning walk, no discussion — it happened almost on autopilot within a few weeks. The days I told myself "I'll train when I feel ready," it quietly died within a month.

Stanford behaviour scientist BJ Fogg frames this even more bluntly with what he calls the Fogg Behavior Model: a behaviour only happens when motivation, ability, and a prompt converge at the same moment. Motivation is the least reliable of the three — it's the one variable you can't summon on demand. Ability and prompt, on the other hand, are things you can engineer. Make the behaviour small enough that ability is never the bottleneck, and anchor it to a rock-solid prompt, and the behaviour fires whether or not you feel like it.

## Building the chain

I stopped trying to fix my motivation years ago. Instead I built chains — one action handing off to the next without a decision in between. The workout doesn't start on its own; it starts the second the morning walk ends. Reading doesn't need willpower; it starts the moment the coffee is poured. Each link removes a decision point, and every decision point removed is one less place for the day to fall apart.

This is also why the comeback after my back injury worked. Three months of bed rest is enough time to lose a program, a rhythm, and most of your confidence. When I restarted, motivation was nowhere in the room — the first squat felt like a betrayal of what my body used to do. What got me from one rep back to a full session wasn't a pep talk. It was re-attaching training to its old anchor point in the day and refusing to renegotiate the trigger, rep by rep, week by week.

## It's not just training

The same chain logic runs through the rest of my routine, not just the workout. Plant-based eating with precise protein tracking isn't a willpower contest three times a day — it's a decision made once, at the planning stage, and then just executed on autopilot at mealtime. Badminton didn't need to compete with running for my motivation either; it sits in a different slot in the chain entirely, a different link for a different kind of reset. The less any single link depends on how I feel when I arrive at it, the more of the day survives intact.

Six months of testing fifty thinkers' philosophies against my actual life taught me the same lesson from a different angle. Ideas that asked me to "want it more" fell apart within a week. Ideas that gave me a specific, repeatable action — attached to a specific moment — kept working on the days I didn't want them to. That pattern repeated often enough that I stopped treating it as a coincidence and started treating it as the actual mechanism behind every version of discipline I've ever seen hold up.

## The takeaway

Stop asking how to get more motivated. Ask what your system is attached to. Pick one habit you keep abandoning, find the moment right before it in your day that already happens without fail, and weld the new behaviour directly onto that moment. No decision, no negotiation — just the next link in the chain.

You don't need to feel ready. You need the chain to already be built by the time you don't.

references.label: Lally et al. — 'How habits are formed' (European Journal of Social Psychology, 2010), via UCL research summary
BJ Fogg — The Fogg Behavior Model (Stanford Behavior Design Lab)

title: Tendon Patience: Why Planche and Maltese Take Years
excerpt: Muscle adapts in weeks. Tendons take years. Why my planche and Maltese progressions move slower than my ego wants, and why that pace is correct.
body:
Everyone wants to know how long it takes to get a planche. Wrong question again — the honest answer depends less on your muscle and almost entirely on tissue most people never think about: your tendons.

Muscle is fast. Give it a few weeks of consistent loading and it visibly responds — more size, more output, a strength curve you can feel improving session to session. Tendons are not fast. They are slow, stubborn, and largely indifferent to your training log.

## The tissue that doesn't care about your timeline

Planche and Maltese aren't strength moves in the way a heavy squat is a strength move. They are leverage moves that place enormous, sustained load through the wrist, elbow, and shoulder tendons — tissue that has to physically remodel its collagen structure to survive that load without breaking down. A 2022 systematic review in Sports Medicine, pulling together dozens of studies on lower-limb tendons under mechanical loading, found that tendons adapt through two separate mechanisms: changes in the material itself — more collagen, better cross-linking — and an increase in the tendon's actual cross-sectional area. The second mechanism, the structural one, is the slow one. Some of the studies in that review found continued tendon stiffness adaptation even after four years of consistent loading in young athletes, with no ceiling in sight.

Four years. Not four weeks. Read that again before you rage-quit your first failed planche lean.

This is precisely why so many calisthenics athletes get hurt chasing a straight-bar planche on a muscle-only timeline. Your shoulders can produce the strength to hold the position long before your tendons can safely absorb the load of holding it. The muscle writes a check the connective tissue can't yet cash.

## What I actually do about it

My approach changed the day I stopped measuring progress in "can I hold this yet" and started measuring it in "did I load this today without pain tomorrow." Progressions — tuck, advanced tuck, straddle, full — exist for exactly this reason: they are not steps for your muscles, they are steps for your tendons to catch up without getting injured trying to skip ahead.

The back injury taught me this lesson at a cost I don't want to pay twice. Three months of bed rest is a brutal audit of every shortcut you took getting there. When I came back, I rebuilt from a single bodyweight squat and refused to rush the reintroduction of load, no matter how good a session felt. Tissue that has been dormant doesn't care that your mind remembers what you used to lift. It has to be re-earned, physically, on its own schedule.

## Where it actually hurts if you rush it

The elbow and wrist take the worst of it in planche training specifically, because the lever gets longer as you progress from tuck toward full — more of your bodyweight sits further from the joint, and the tendons crossing the elbow are exactly the tissue least forgiving of a rushed timeline. It's a slow, low-grade ache at first, easy to train through, which is exactly what makes it dangerous. Muscle soreness tells you to rest for a day. Tendon irritation tells you to rest for a month, and it tells you quietly enough that most people don't listen until it's screaming.

I run badminton and long-distance running alongside the strength work partly for this reason — they load a different profile of connective tissue at a different rhythm, so the wrist and elbow tendons doing the leverage work get real recovery windows instead of daily accumulating stress with zero deload. Cross-training isn't cross-training for the muscle here. It's active rest for the tendon.

## Patience as a training variable

I now treat tendon adaptation as a training variable with the same respect I give sets and reps. That means longer holds at lower intensities before adding lean angle, deliberate deload weeks even when I feel strong, and total refusal to let a good day override a slow plan. The plan is slow on purpose. It is slow because the tissue underneath the muscle is slow, and no amount of willpower changes collagen synthesis rates.

This is also where tendon patience and the 40% rule diverge in an important way. The 40% rule is about pushing past a mental quit signal within a single set. Tendon patience is closer to the opposite instinct — knowing when NOT to push, because the thing limiting you isn't your nervous system lying to you, it's tissue telling the truth. Learning to tell the difference between "my mind is quitting early" and "my tendon needs another six months" is arguably the single most important skill in advanced calisthenics.

## Reading the signal correctly

The hardest skill in this entire process is learning to distinguish two signals that feel almost identical in the moment: general fatigue, which you should push through, and specific joint or tendon irritation, which you should not. I use a simple filter now — if the discomfort is diffuse, symmetric, and fades within the first few minutes of moving, it's fatigue, and the 40% rule applies. If it's localized to one spot, sharper than the surrounding soreness, or still there the next morning in the exact same place, it's a tendon telling me to back off, not a mind lying to me.

Getting that filter wrong in either direction is expensive. Treat every ache as a tendon warning and you'll never progress past the tuck. Treat every tendon warning as a mind games and you'll be back on bed rest inside a year. I know, because I've done both.

## The takeaway

If you're chasing planche, Maltese, or any advanced lever, stop timing your progress against your muscles. Time it against your tendons instead. Give every progression step months, not weeks, load it consistently, back off the instant something feels sharp instead of tired, and accept that the slowest tissue in the chain sets the pace for the whole system. Rushing it doesn't get you there faster. It just gets you back to bed rest.

references.label: Mechanical, Material and Morphological Adaptations of Healthy Lower Limb Tendons to Mechanical Loading — Systematic Review & Meta-Analysis (Sports Medicine, 2022)

title: Local-First AI: Why I Run My Models On-Device
excerpt: Every major AI app wants your data on someone else’s server. BrainBox does not. Why I built it to run entirely on the device in your pocket.
body:
Most AI apps today have a quiet, unspoken deal built into them: send us your conversations, your habits, your journal entries, and we'll process them somewhere in a data center you'll never see. You don't get to opt out of the deal — it's the deal.

I built BrainBox specifically to break that deal. It's a local-first AI lifestyle app — on-device chat, personas, habit and routine tracking, journaling — where the model runs on your phone, not in my cloud, because there is no cloud in the loop at all.

## Why local, not just 'privacy-friendly cloud'

'Privacy-friendly cloud' is a marketing phrase, not an architecture. If your data ever leaves the device, it exists somewhere else, under someone else's control, subject to someone else's breach, someone else's subpoena, someone else's outage. Local-first isn't a friendlier version of cloud-first. It's a different category of trust model — the kind where the honest answer to "where does my data go" is "nowhere, it never left."

There's also a much less philosophical reason: latency and reliability. A model running on-device answers the moment you ask, no round trip to a server, no spinner, no dependency on whether your signal has three bars. Offline-first isn't a nice-to-have feature bullet. On a train, in a basement gym, at 3am with the wifi down, it's the difference between the app working and the app being furniture.

## The technology that makes this possible now

This wasn't really practical a few years ago. What changed is the ecosystem around llama.cpp and the GGUF model format — an open-source inference engine, originally built by Georgi Gerganov, that runs large language models efficiently in plain C/C++ across ordinary consumer hardware, no server farm required. GGUF is the compressed, quantized file format that makes a model small enough to actually live on a phone instead of a rack in a data center. That project, plus tools like llama.rn for React Native and Ollama for local model management, are the quiet infrastructure that made it possible for someone like me — one engineer, no cloud budget — to ship real on-device inference inside a consumer app instead of just writing a wrapper around someone else's API.

BrainBox uses this stack directly: llama.rn and GGUF-format models running the on-device chat and persona layer, WatermelonDB handling all data storage locally so the app works fully offline, and computer vision components that never need to phone home either. Nothing about the core experience requires a network request to function.

## Problem, built, stack, status

I try to think about every project the same way: what problem, what got built, what stack, what's the actual status — not the pitch-deck version. The problem here was simple: I wanted an AI companion for chat, personas, habit tracking, and journaling that didn't quietly become a data pipeline pointed at someone else's server. What got built is BrainBox — on-device LLM chat through llama.rn and GGUF models, a habit and routine tracker, a journaling layer, and a gamified wellness engine rendered with React Native Skia at 60fps, all sitting on top of WatermelonDB for fully offline data storage, with computer vision components running locally too. The status isn't a pitch either — it's live on Google Play, shipped, being used.

Building all of that as one person, without a cloud budget or a backend team, only worked because none of the pieces required a server round-trip to function. Habit tracking doesn't need the network. Journaling doesn't need the network. Even the chat doesn't need it, because the model lives on the same device as the data it's reasoning about. Every piece of the stack was chosen specifically because it could run without asking permission from a server somewhere else.

## Sovereignty over convenience

I'll be honest about the trade-off, because pretending there isn't one would be dishonest. On-device models are smaller and less capable than the giant frontier models running on cloud GPUs. You give up some raw intelligence for full control of your own data. I make that trade deliberately, and I built BrainBox for people who'd make the same one — the same instinct that has me running Ubuntu as a daily driver instead of a locked-down consumer OS, and reaching for physical books instead of always defaulting to a screen. Convenience is real. So is the cost of trading your privacy for it, quietly, one API call at a time.

## Why a generalist background actually helps here

Shipping something like this end to end isn't really a single-skill job, and I don't think it could have come from a narrow specialist. The on-device model layer needed the same mindset as the Kotlin-based automation work I've built separately; the offline data layer needed the same discipline as any backend system, just inverted to live on the client; the wellness engine's animation layer needed frontend and motion sensibility as much as engineering. None of those are the same skill, but all of them had to agree with each other inside one small app running on one phone with no server to fall back on when something didn't fit.

That's the actual argument for generalism I keep coming back to: specialists build excellent pieces. Someone still has to be in the room who understands enough of every piece to make them agree to run together, offline, on hardware you don't control the specs of. That was my job on this build, and it's the same job on most of what I ship.

## The takeaway

Before you install the next AI app, ask one question: where does my data actually go when I hit send? If the honest answer involves a server you don't control, that's a choice being made for you, not by you. Local-first AI isn't about distrust for its own sake — it's about putting the decision back where it belongs.

references.label: llama.cpp — LLM inference in C/C++ (GGUF format, ggml-org)

title: Meditation Is Debugging for the Mind
excerpt: I did not come to meditation for peace of mind as a slogan. I came to it as an engineer looking for the bug in my own head. What temple practice actually does.
body:
I approach meditation the same way I approach a broken build: something is misbehaving, and I need to find where, exactly, the process breaks down. Not as a mood. As a debugging pass on the human operating system.

That framing isn't a metaphor I picked for the aesthetics. It's the literal way I use temple practice — sit, observe the process, find where the reaction is firing instead of the action, patch it, repeat tomorrow.

## The bug: reaction pretending to be action

Most days run on autopilot. Something happens, a feeling fires, a response follows — and the whole loop executes in milliseconds with zero inspection. That's a reaction: your circumstances pulling a lever you didn't know was exposed. An action is the same lever, pulled on purpose, by you, with intention.

Meditation, in temple practice, is the only tool I've found that reliably slows that loop down enough to see it. Sitting still with nowhere to direct your attention forces the reaction to surface in slow motion — you watch the irritation arrive, watch the urge to snap at someone build, and for the first time you get a few hundred milliseconds of daylight between the trigger and your usual response. That gap is the entire product. Everything else is a side effect.

## What the research shows, and what it doesn't

I don't need a study to know silence changed how I think — I have years of it in my own body. But I also don't think "trust me" is good enough when I'm asking someone else to spend their time on this, so here's what's actually been measured.

A 2005 study led by Sara Lazar at Massachusetts General Hospital used MRI to compare experienced meditators against matched non-meditators and found measurably thicker cortex in regions tied to attention and sensory processing — the first structural evidence that sustained meditation practice is associated with physical changes in the brain, not just a reported feeling of calm. It doesn't prove causation from a single study, and the sample was small, but it's real, peer-reviewed, and it lines up with what a decade of subsequent research kept finding.

Zoom out further and a 2014 systematic review and meta-analysis in JAMA Internal Medicine, pooling dozens of trials across thousands of participants, found that meditation programs produced small-to-moderate but consistent reductions in anxiety, depression, and general psychological stress compared to control groups. Small-to-moderate, not miraculous. That's an honest number, and I trust honest numbers more than testimonials — including my own.

## Growing in silence

I found my way to temple practice during a period when I'd deliberately stepped back from most external noise — no TV, no movies, no sports entertainment, most of my attention going into building skills nobody was watching me build. That silence wasn't empty. It's where the spiritual side of this actually took root, long before I ever talked about it publicly. I wanted to grow in the silence before exhibiting the process, and meditation was the practice that made the silence usable instead of just quiet.

That period is also where I first noticed the debugging effect directly, well before I had language for it. Sitting in a temple, doing nothing external at all, I'd watch my own mind generate a complaint, a craving, an urge to check something, entirely on its own — and just observing that generation, without acting on it, was the first time I actually saw the reaction happening instead of just being swept along by it.

## Why temple, not app

I do this in temple practice specifically, not through a guided app with a voice in my ear. Silence without narration is the point — an app filling the space with instructions is still noise, just quieter noise. The debugging only works if you're actually alone with the process, not being walked through someone else's script of what you should be noticing.

This is also where the discipline of meditation and the discipline of training rhyme. Both are exercises in refusing to react to the first signal your body or mind sends you. The gym teaches you your mind quits around forty percent of true capacity. The temple teaches you the same lesson from the other direction — that the emotional reaction firing right now is not the full picture either, just the loudest one.

## Debugging is never a one-time fix

The metaphor holds up in one more uncomfortable way: debugging isn't a single session that fixes the system forever. You patch one bug, ship it, and a different one surfaces next week, because the underlying system keeps running and keeps generating new edge cases. Meditation works the same way. There is no session after which the reactive loop is permanently closed. There's only the daily practice of catching it again, a little faster than yesterday, in a slightly different disguise than yesterday's version wore.

I stopped expecting a finish line on this a long time ago. Some days the gap between trigger and response is wide and I catch the reaction cleanly before it fires. Other days it's narrow and something slips through anyway. Neither day is a verdict on whether the practice works. It's just today's build, with today's bugs, waiting for tomorrow's session.

## The takeaway

You don't need an hour, a cushion, or a subscription. You need ten minutes of actual silence and the discipline to sit through the discomfort of not reacting to the first thing your mind throws at you. Watch the gap between trigger and response. That gap, widened slightly every day, is the entire practice.

references.label: Lazar et al. — Meditation Experience Is Associated with Increased Cortical Thickness (NeuroReport, 2005)
Goyal et al. — Meditation Programs for Psychological Stress and Well-being: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis (JAMA Internal Medicine, 2014)

title: 50 Thinkers, 6 Months: What Survived Contact With Real Life
excerpt: I spent six months testing philosophy against real life instead of just reading it. Most of it did not survive. What that process actually teaches, and what it does not.
body:
On January 1st I set myself one rule: gather the core life philosophies of roughly fifty thinkers, and instead of reading them, test them. In workouts. In work. In the boring Tuesday afternoons where philosophy either earns its keep or it doesn't.

Six months later the honest scoreboard is that most of it didn't survive. That's not a takedown of the fifty thinkers — it's what happens when you actually run an idea through the friction of a real week instead of nodding along to it in a book.

## Testing a philosophy is not reading a philosophy

There's a long, serious tradition behind the idea that philosophy was never meant to be a purely intellectual exercise. The historian Pierre Hadot argued in Philosophy as a Way of Life that for the ancient world — Socrates through the Stoics through early Christianity — philosophy wasn't a subject you studied, it was a set of practices you did: exercises for reshaping how you actually perceive and respond to your own life, not essays for a seminar. Reading is passive. A "spiritual exercise," his term for it, only exists in the doing.

That's the exact distinction I built my experiment around, whether or not I'd have used his language for it at the time. An idea about discipline is worthless until it's been run through an actual set of push-ups on a night you didn't want to move. An idea about detachment is worthless until it's been tested against a genuinely frustrating conversation with someone you love. Reading tells you what a thinker believed. Testing tells you whether the belief holds weight.

## What a real test actually looks like

The test isn't vague. For each idea, I picked a specific, repeatable situation — a training session, a work deadline, a family dinner — and I applied the idea there, on purpose, and watched what happened compared to my default behaviour. Did it change the outcome? Did it change how I felt about the outcome? Did it survive a bad day, or only a good one? Ideas that only work when you're already in a good mood aren't systems. They're decoration.

Most philosophies failed this filter quietly. Not dramatically — they just turned out to require conditions real life doesn't reliably provide: perfect information, unlimited time, an audience that behaves rationally. A handful survived because they didn't ask for any of that. They worked exactly as well on a bad Tuesday as a good one.

## The one result I will name

I'll break my own rule for exactly one, because it's the foundation everything else got built on: separating action from reaction. A reaction is your circumstances pulling your strings. An action is you, deciding, with intention, regardless of what just happened to you. Before the experiment I lived most weeks almost entirely in reaction — replying to whatever the day threw at me. The test I still run today, multiple times a day, is one question: am I acting with intention right now, or just reacting to the moment? That single filter changed more of my behaviour than the other forty-nine philosophies combined, which is exactly why it's the one I'm willing to name specifically instead of leaving in the general pile.

## The general lesson, not the specific scorecard

I'm not going to hand you a numbered list of which five thinkers "won," because a scoreboard like that invites you to skip the actual work and just adopt my conclusions secondhand — which defeats the entire point of the experiment. The value was never in the answer. It was in building the habit of treating every idea as a hypothesis instead of a belief.

What I can tell you, honestly, is the shape of what separates a survivor from a casualty: survivors gave me something to do, immediately, in a specific moment, not something to feel or believe in the abstract. The idea that changed how I train did it by giving me a concrete action for the exact moment I wanted to quit a set. The idea that changed how I argue did it by giving me a specific pause to insert before I responded, not a general instruction to "be more patient."

## Preparedness over prediction

Running an experiment like this for six months, on myself, with no guarantee any of it would work, only made sense under one operating principle I hold above most others: preparedness over prediction. I couldn't predict in January which ideas would survive contact with July. Nobody can, honestly, no matter how convincing a book sounds while you're reading it in isolation. What I could do was prepare a fair test for each one and let the result be whatever it actually was, including "this doesn't work for me," which is a perfectly valid result that most people are too invested in a thinker's reputation to accept.

That's the real discipline underneath the whole experiment, more than any individual idea that survived it: building the habit of testing instead of predicting, in every part of life, not just the fifty books on a shelf.

## The takeaway

Stop collecting philosophies like trophies. Pick one idea you already nod along to, find the next real situation where it applies, and run it — on purpose, with a way to check afterward whether it actually changed your behaviour or just your vocabulary. If it only survives in conversation, it isn't a philosophy yet. It's a quote.

references.label: Pierre Hadot — Philosophy as a Way of Life: Spiritual Exercises from Socrates to Foucault

title: ADHD Taught Me to Stop Trusting My Memory
excerpt: I struggled academically as a kid, easily distracted, always the one who forgot. What actually fixed it was not willpower. It was building an environment that remembers instead of me.
body:
I was the kid who forgot the homework, forgot the deadline, forgot halfway through a sentence what he was originally trying to say. ADHD, undiagnosed drama aside, meant my academic years looked like a long string of good intentions with nothing behind them.

What eventually worked wasn't a discovery about willpower. It was giving up on willpower as the plan entirely, and building an environment that did the remembering for me instead.

## The part people get wrong about ADHD

The lazy read on ADHD is a motivation problem — that the person just needs to try harder, care more, focus better. Clinical psychologist Russell Barkley, whose decades of research reframed how ADHD is understood, argues the opposite: ADHD isn't a knowledge deficit or a motivation deficit, it's an executive function deficit. Barkley identifies specific executive functions people use to regulate themselves day to day — self-restraint, time management, self-organisation, self-motivation, emotional regulation — and the core impairment in ADHD isn't that a person doesn't know what to do. It's that the internal system responsible for holding onto that intention long enough to act on it isn't reliable.

Read that twice if you grew up being told you "just needed to focus." Knowing what to do was never the missing piece. Holding onto it across time, without external scaffolding, was.

## What changed everything: stop relying on the part that's broken

Once I stopped treating memory and in-the-moment self-discipline as tools I could rely on, and started treating them as unreliable components I needed to route around, the whole problem became solvable. Not through more discipline. Through engineering.

I taught myself, across game dev, app dev, frontend, mathematics, and a pile of other domains, largely because traditional structured learning — sit still, absorb, remember, recall on demand — was fighting against exactly the part of my brain that doesn't cooperate. Self-teaching let me build my own scaffolding: project-based, visible, broken into pieces small enough that I didn't need to hold the whole thing in my head to make progress on any one piece.

That habit became the template for everything after. If a task depends on me remembering it unprompted at the right moment, I've already lost. So the task gets externalised — a trigger, a checklist, a physical anchor in my day — until the environment carries the weight my working memory can't.

## Why hackathons worked when classrooms didn't

The irony is that the same brain that couldn't reliably remember a homework deadline could sit through 10–11 hours in a single day building something, and win hackathons doing it. Classrooms asked me to hold abstract information in my head with no external structure and produce it on demand later. Hackathons handed me a concrete problem, a visible deadline, and immediate feedback on every attempt — the environment did half the regulating that my own head couldn't. That contrast is exactly where I learned, years before I had Barkley's framework to explain it, that the fix was never going to be "try harder to remember." It was going to be "stop needing to."

Avoiding TV, movies, and mainstream entertainment during that era wasn't discipline for its own sake either — it was one more piece of environment design. Less unstructured input meant less competing for the same limited attention I needed for the scaffolding to hold. Every environment change was really the same move: remove reliance on a part of the system that wasn't reliable, replace it with something external that was.

## What the environment actually looks like

Concretely: habits get physically attached to an anchor moment, not to willpower — training starts the second the walk ends, no decision required. Routines get written down outside my head the moment they're decided, not trusted to memory. Big goals get broken into pieces small enough that starting doesn't require recalling the whole plan, just the next physical action in front of me.

None of this is a productivity hack I picked up from a book. It's a survival system I built as a kid who kept failing the version of school that assumed everyone's internal memory and self-discipline worked the same way, and it just kept getting more refined as the stakes got higher — hackathons, then real production systems, then an app built to run entirely offline on someone's phone.

## The generalist side-effect

Building my own scaffolding across so many different domains — game dev, app dev, frontend, mathematics, 3D modelling, crafts — wasn't really a decision to "become a generalist" in the identity sense people mean it now. It was a side effect of needing a workaround for one specific problem, applied over and over to whatever I happened to be learning that month. Each new domain got the same treatment: break it into pieces small enough that memory wasn't load-bearing, externalize the plan, attach the next action to something that already happens.

Years later that repeated workaround is most of what people now call being a generalist by design. It didn't start as a philosophy. It started as the only version of learning that worked for a kid whose brain wouldn't reliably hold onto anything it wasn't actively doing something with, right now, in front of him.

## The takeaway

If you keep "forgetting" the thing you swore you'd do, stop trying to fix your memory. Fix your environment instead. Attach the behaviour to something that already happens without fail, write down what your brain won't reliably hold, and shrink the task until starting it doesn't require remembering the whole plan — just the next single action in front of you. The system remembers. You just have to follow it.

references.label: Russell Barkley — Executive Functioning and Self-Regulation in ADHD
